summary: I served in the United States Cadet Nurse Corps during World War II. I joined after working three years at the Detroit Edison Company, seeking a better opportunity. The Corps trained young women from various backgrounds to become registered nurses through a three-year program that combined classes and hospital work, such as at Harper Hospital in Detroit. We didn’t have military ranks or pins, just our school pin and diploma, and the government fully supported our training. The Corps was a wartime program that ended after those enrolled finished their courses.

Before joining the United States Cadet Nurse Corps, I worked for three years at the Detroit Edison Company in electrical service. It wasn’t related to the medical field, but it was my first job after high school. I decided to leave that job when I heard about the Cadet Nurse Corps and thought I could do better by training as a nurse. My time there was important as it gave me work experience before I changed careers.

I trained at Harper Hospital in Detroit as part of my nursing education with the United States Cadet Nurse Corps. Our days were split between classes and working with patients to complete an eight-hour day. Many of the nurses had gone into service, so students like me kept the hospital running during the war. After finishing my training, I returned to Harper Hospital for about nine months before moving on to other nursing positions.

During the last six months of my nursing training, I worked at the Veterans Hospital in Maywood, Illinois. This was a choice offered to us, and I selected to work with veterans, many of whom were from World War II and some from World War I. Maywood was a significant place for me because it was where I gained experience caring for disabled veterans, including a paraplegic patient who painted with his teeth. The hospital remains important, though smaller now, as Loyola University Chicago has taken over some buildings there.
cues: World War II and World War I veterans

summary: I spent the final six months of my nursing training at Hines Veterans Hospital near Maywood, Illinois, in 1947. There, I cared for veterans from both World War II and World War I, including a memorable paraplegic patient who painted with his mouth. Hines was a critical facility for veterans' care, and although it has since become smaller, it was an important part of my nursing education and early career.
cues: World War I and II veterans

summary: World War II was the backdrop for my nursing training and service. The war created a shortage of registered nurses as many enlisted, so the United States Cadet Nurse Corps was established to train more nurses quickly. I was in training during the war’s final years and graduated in 1947, after the war ended in 1945. I remember hearing the news of the war’s end while working in the hospital, and there was much excitement among staff and patients alike.

During my time at Hines Veterans Hospital, I cared for veterans from both World War II and World War I. Some of the older veterans had served in World War I, and their presence reminded me of the long legacy of service these men had given. It was humbling to work alongside those who had fought in both world wars, and it gave me a deeper appreciation for the sacrifices made over many years.

Loyola University Chicago now occupies some of the buildings at the former Hines Veterans Hospital site in Maywood, Illinois. When I was training there in 1947, it was still a large veterans hospital, but over time the facility has become smaller as Loyola expanded its presence. This change reflects the evolving nature of veteran care and the university's growth in the area.

I grew up in Mount Clemens, Michigan, which is about 20 miles north of Detroit. It was where I attended high school before working at Detroit Edison Company and later joining the United States Cadet Nurse Corps. Returning to my high school for private chemistry lessons was part of my preparation for nursing school, showing how my roots in Mount Clemens helped shape my career path.

Detroit was central to my early adult life and nursing training. I worked at Detroit Edison Company before entering the United States Cadet Nurse Corps, and I trained at Harper Hospital in Detroit. The city had several large hospitals with training programs, including Henry Ford Hospital where a friend trained. Detroit also hosted reunions for our nurse corps group, reflecting the strong community we built there.

Henry Ford Hospital in Detroit was one of the major hospitals that had a United States Cadet Nurse Corps training program, similar to where I trained at Harper Hospital. Although I did not train there myself, a friend of mine completed her training at Henry Ford Hospital. It was part of the network of large Detroit hospitals that supported the wartime nursing effort.

Indianapolis was one of the cities with a veterans hospital where Cadet Nurse Corps trainees could be assigned for their veteran nursing experience. While I was sent to Hines Veterans Hospital near Maywood, Illinois, I know that some of my classmates were sent to Indianapolis. These placements were part of a national system to provide nursing care to veterans across the United States.

After completing my required service at Hines Veterans Hospital and returning briefly to Harper Hospital, I took a nursing position in Colorado. This move was unrelated to veterans' care but allowed me to continue my nursing career in a new location. It marked a new chapter for me after my wartime and immediate postwar nursing experiences in the Midwest.

I worked in Pueblo, Colorado after finishing my nursing training and service in Illinois. This was part of my continued nursing career outside of the veterans’ hospital system. Moving to Pueblo represented a fresh start in a different part of the country following my formative years in the Midwest during and after World War II.

During World War II, many registered nurses joined the Army or Navy, which created a shortage of nurses at home. My sisters worked in automobile factories making parts for the Army and Navy, supporting the war effort on the home front. Although I was in the United States Cadet Nurse Corps and did not serve overseas or in the Army directly, the Army’s needs influenced the creation of the Corps and my training.

Like the Army, the Navy also drew many nurses into service during World War II, contributing to the shortage of nurses in civilian hospitals. My sisters helped by working in factories producing parts for the Navy and Army, supporting the military’s needs. Although I served in the United States Cadet Nurse Corps and did not join the Navy, their demand for nurses was part of the reason the Corps was established.
